verses.text: Now Benjamin begat Bela his firstborn, Ashbel the second, and Aharah the third, Nohah the fourth, and Rapha the fifth. And the sons of Bela were, Addar, and Gera, and Abihud, And Abishua, and Naaman, and Ahoah, And Gera, and Shephuphan, and Huram. And these are the sons of Ehud: these are the heads of the fathers of the inhabitants of Geba, and they removed them to Manahath: And Naaman, and Ahiah, and Gera, he removed them, and begat Uzza, and Ahihud. Guidance: The Chronicler returns to Benjamin and traces its families in detail — far more attention than most tribes receive — beginning with Benjamin's sons and the line of Bela, and the heads of the fathers who dwelt in Geba and were resettled at Manahath. Benjamin is given this expanded treatment for good reason: it was, with Judah, one of the two tribes that remained faithful to the house of David and returned from exile to form the post-exilic community; and it was the tribe of Saul, Israel's first king, whose house this chapter will trace to its climax. The chapter is showing us the Chronicler's special care to preserve Benjamin's records fully, because Benjamin was central to the identity of the people he wrote for. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD weaves particular tribes into His purposes in particular ways — Benjamin, the smallest and once nearly-destroyed tribe, became one of the pillars of the restored nation. The mention of families being 'removed' from one place to another reminds us that even within the land, the lives of God's people were marked by movement and resettlement, all under the providence of God who appoints the bounds of His people's habitation. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD keeps careful track of His people through every migration and resettlement; not a family was lost from His reckoning, even as they moved from Geba to Manahath. The patient detail of Benjamin's genealogy testifies to the LORD's faithful remembrance of a tribe He had preserved and would yet use greatly.

And Shaharaim begat children in the country of Moab, after he had sent them away; Hushim and Baara were his wives. And he begat of Hodesh his wife, Jobab, and Zibia, and Mesha, and Malcham, And Jeuz, and Shachia, and Mirma. These were his sons, heads of the fathers. And of Hushim he begat Abitub, and Elpaal. The sons of Elpaal; Eber, and Misham, and Shamed, who built Ono, and Lod, with the towns thereof. Guidance: The Chronicler records the family of Shaharaim, who 'begat children in the country of Moab,' and notes that his descendants Shamed (Shemed) 'built Ono, and Lod, with the towns thereof.' These towns — Ono and Lod, in the coastal plain northwest of Jerusalem — would still be inhabited by Benjamites after the return from exile (Neh 11:35). The chapter is showing us the building and settling work of Benjamin's families, founding towns that endured for centuries and were resettled by the returning exiles. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's people are builders — establishing towns, planting communities, doing the patient constructive work that outlasts a single generation. The note that these were built by Benjamites and later re-inhabited by the post-exilic community links the Chronicler's readers directly to their ancestors: the very towns they lived in had been founded by the fathers whose names are here recorded. The chapter is teaching us that there is honour in the ordinary work of building and settling; the men who 'built Ono and Lod' are remembered in Scripture for their constructive labour. The chapter is teaching the returned exiles that they stood on ground prepared by faithful forefathers, dwelling in towns their own people had built — a continuity of the covenant community across the centuries, preserved by the providence of God who plants His people and gives them a place to dwell.

Beriah also, and Shema, who were heads of the fathers of the inhabitants of Aijalon, who drove away the inhabitants of Gath: And Ahio, Shashak, and Jeremoth, And Zebadiah, and Arad, and Ader, And Michael, and Ispah, and Joha, the sons of Beriah; And Zebadiah, and Meshullam, and Hezeki, and Heber, Ishmerai also, and Jezliah, and Jobab, the sons of Elpaal; And Jakim, and Zichri, and Zabdi, And Elienai, and Zilthai, and Eliel, And Adaiah, and Beraiah, and Shimrath, the sons of Shimhi; And Ishpan, and Heber, and Eliel, And Abdon, and Zichri, and Hanan, And Hananiah, and Elam, and Antothijah, And Iphedeiah, and Penuel, the sons of Shashak; And Shamsherai, and Shehariah, and Athaliah, And Jaresiah, and Eliah, and Zichri, the sons of Jeroham. These were heads of the fathers, by their generations, chief men. These dwelt in Jerusalem. Guidance: The Chronicler records many heads of Benjamin's families, noting that Beriah and Shema 'drove away the inhabitants of Gath,' and concluding that all these were 'chief men' who 'dwelt in Jerusalem.' That closing note is significant: Benjamin's territory bordered Jerusalem, and indeed part of the holy city lay within Benjamin's allotment, so that Benjamites dwelt in Jerusalem alongside Judah. The chapter is showing us that the chief men of Benjamin had a share in the LORD's chosen city; they lived where the temple stood, near the heart of Israel's worship. The chapter is teaching us the privilege of dwelling near the house of God — these Benjamite families had their homes in Jerusalem, the city the LORD had chosen to put His name. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community, who were repopulating Jerusalem after the return (a hard task, for many were reluctant to settle in the ruined city, Neh 11:1-2), that their Benjamite forefathers had long dwelt there as 'chief men.' To live in Jerusalem was an honour rooted in their tribal heritage. The chapter is teaching us that proximity to the LORD's house and city is a privilege to be valued; the Benjamites who dwelt in Jerusalem stood near the center of God's dealings with His people. And the note that they 'drove away the inhabitants of Gath' shows them as a tribe of action and courage, contending for their place in the land. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's people are called both to dwell near Him and to stand firm for the inheritance He has given.

And at Gibeon dwelt the father of Gibeon; whose wife's name was Maachah: And his firstborn son Abdon, and Zur, and Kish, and Baal, and Nadab, And Gedor, and Ahio, and Zacher. And Mikloth begat Shimeah. And these also dwelt with their brethren in Jerusalem, over against them. Guidance: The Chronicler now records the family that dwelt at Gibeon — 'the father of Gibeon' and his wife Maachah and their sons, including a Kish and a Baal and a Nadab. This is the family from which Saul would come, the ancestors of Israel's first king, settled at Gibeon (an important high place where the tabernacle would later stand, 1 Chron 16:39; 21:29). The chapter is showing us the Chronicler deliberately setting the stage for the house of Saul, tracing the royal Benjamite family back to its roots at Gibeon. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD prepares the way for the great figures of His history long before they appear; the family at Gibeon, dwelling quietly among their brethren, was the seedbed from which the first king of Israel would spring. The chapter is teaching us that no family knows what the LORD may bring forth from it; from this household at Gibeon came a king. The note that they too 'dwelt with their brethren in Jerusalem' binds Saul's ancestral family to the holy city even before Saul's reign. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's providence works through ordinary families settled in ordinary places, quietly preparing the people and the lineage through whom He will act. As the genealogy narrows toward Saul, we are reminded that even the king whose reign would end in failure came from a family the LORD had placed and preserved — for the LORD governs the origins of all, both those who prove faithful and those who do not.

And Mikloth begat Shimeah. And these also dwelt with their brethren in Jerusalem, over against them. And Ner begat Kish, and Kish begat Saul, and Saul begat Jonathan, and Malchishua, and Abinadab, and Eshbaal. And the son of Jonathan was Meribbaal; and Meribbaal begat Micah. And the sons of Micah were, Pithon, and Melech, and Tarea, and Ahaz. And Ahaz begat Jehoadah; and Jehoadah begat Alemeth, and Azmaveth, and Zimri; and Zimri begat Moza, And Moza begat Binea: Rapha was his son, Eleasah his son, Azel his son: And Azel had six sons, whose names are these, Azrikam, Bocheru, and Ishmael, and Sheariah, and Obadiah, and Hanan. All these were the sons of Azel. And the sons of Eshek his brother were, Ulam his firstborn, Jehush the second, and Eliphelet the third. And the sons of Ulam were mighty men of valour, archers, and had many sons, and sons' sons, an hundred and fifty. All these are of the sons of Benjamin. Guidance: The genealogy reaches its climax: the house of Saul. 'Kish begat Saul, and Saul begat Jonathan... and the son of Jonathan was Meribbaal' (that is, Mephibosheth, the lame son whom David sought out and showed kindness for Jonathan's sake, 2 Sam 9), 'and Meribbaal begat Micah' — and the line runs on for many generations down to Azel and beyond, ending with the 'mighty men of valour, archers' of the sons of Ulam. The chapter is showing us the remarkable fact that the Chronicler carefully preserves the line of Saul — the king whom the LORD rejected, whose dynasty did not endure, whose place was given to David. Though Saul's house lost the throne, the Chronicler does not erase it; he records it with honour, tracing it down through Jonathan's faithful line for fourteen or more generations. The chapter is teaching us the grace and faithfulness woven even into the record of a rejected house. Saul failed and his kingdom passed away, yet his descendants were not blotted out — and especially the line of Jonathan, David's beloved friend, was preserved and honoured. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's covenant love reaches beyond the rejected king to the faithful son: Jonathan, who loved David and submitted to God's choice of him, has his line carefully recorded, and his son Mephibosheth was cherished at David's own table. The chapter is teaching us that human failure does not put a family beyond the reach of God's remembrance; the house of Saul, though it forfeited the crown, kept its place in the genealogy of Israel and produced mighty men of valour for generations. And there is a deeper lesson: the throne was not Saul's to keep but the LORD's to give, and He gave it to David and to David's greater Son. Yet even the house that lost the throne was not cast away from the people of God. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is faithful to remember all His people — the chosen and the set-aside alike — and that His grace, supremely shown to David, overflowed even to the household of the king He had rejected, for the sake of the love between David and Jonathan that foreshadowed a greater covenant love still.
